commercialization_path.acquirer_value: A frontier lab, AI coding platform, cloud provider, or security vendor gets a clear exposure-management wedge for MCP and agentic AI that complements secure context retrieval.
commercialization_path.enterprise_layer: Hosted exposure graph with runtime event ingestion, path diffing, per-agent access review, policy recommendations, and trust-center API exports.
commercialization_path.open_layer: Generated exposure graph from public SecurityRecipes evidence packs, workflow manifests, policy, identity, and context artifacts.
commercialization_path.team_layer: Private graph overlays for tenant workflows, customer MCP servers, source-host permissions, approval systems, connector drift, and incident evidence.

enterprise_adoption_packet.board_level_claim: SecurityRecipes can show the action graph behind agentic AI: context, identities, tools, data boundaries, risk, and evidence are inspectable before scale.
default_questions_answered: Which agent identities create the most exposure?
Which workflows combine high residual risk with write or approval-required MCP namespaces?
Which MCP namespaces are pilot, tenant-sensitive, or missing production controls?
Which context sources and hashes are attached to each path?
Which evidence pack proves a path can be reconstructed after an incident?
enterprise_adoption_packet.recommended_first_use: Use the graph during AI platform intake, MCP connector approval, quarterly agent-access review, trust-center export, and acquisition diligence.
enterprise_adoption_packet.sales_motion: Lead with open graph evidence, then sell hosted graph ingestion from live MCP gateway logs, IAM events, source-host reviews, approval records, and customer-private context registries.

global_rules: Every exposure path must bind workflow, identity, context package, MCP namespace, authorization decision, egress policy, and receipt evidence.
Unknown MCP namespaces, unregistered context, missing identity, missing authorization, and missing egress policy are treated as graph gaps.
Write, approval-required, pilot, tenant-sensitive, and high residual-risk paths receive higher exposure scores.
Generated exposure scores are planning and diligence evidence; runtime enforcement still belongs in the MCP gateway, IAM layer, and approval system.

risk_score_thresholds.meaning: Path may run under normal gateway audit, run receipts, and drift checks.
Path may run inside a bounded rollout with workflow-owner review and current evidence.
Path needs workflow owner or security architecture review before expansion.
Path has high-impact or approval-required exposure and needs explicit architecture review before scale.

intent: Define the SecurityRecipes agentic exposure graph: a machine-readable relationship map that joins workflows, secure-context sources, non-human identities, MCP namespaces, connector trust, authorization decisions, egress boundaries, readiness, capability risk, and run receipts into risk-ranked paths before agents act.

nodes.label: MCP Connector Trust Registry and Pack
Base Image and Container Layer Remediation

path_classes.question: Which agents can combine approved secure context with read-only MCP namespaces?
path_classes.title: Context to Read Tool

path_classes.question: Which agents can convert retrieved context into scoped branch, ticket, or evidence writes?
path_classes.title: Context to Write Tool

path_classes.question: Which paths stage or request high-impact MCP operations before a human approval point?
path_classes.title: Approval-Required Tool Path

path_classes.question: Which paths can mix customer-sensitive context with model, MCP, telemetry, or external destination boundaries?
path_classes.title: Tenant-Sensitive Context Path

path_classes.question: Which paths join high residual-risk workflows with write, approval, financial, chain, identity, or production-impact namespaces?
path_classes.title: High-Impact Authority Path

positioning.buyer: AI Platform, Security Architecture, Product Security, AppSec, IAM, GRC, procurement security, model-risk, and acquisition diligence teams
positioning.category: Agentic exposure management for secure context and MCP tool use
positioning.enterprise_promise: SecurityRecipes can answer which agent, acting through which identity, may retrieve which context, call which MCP namespace, cross which data boundary, and produce which evidence before an enterprise approves scale.
positioning.why_now: Agentic AI systems are shifting from chat assistants into autonomous actors that combine retrieved context, MCP tools, A2A handoffs, non-human identities, and long-running workflows. Buyers need exposure visibility across the action graph, not isolated prompt guidance.

residual_risks.risk: The graph proves source-controlled reference exposure, not live customer runtime behavior.
residual_risks.treatment: Bind hosted deployments to MCP gateway logs, IAM issuance events, approval records, source-host reviews, tenant context registries, and sealed run receipts.

residual_risks.risk: Risk scores can become stale after model, prompt, connector, workflow, identity, policy, or context drift.
residual_risks.treatment: Regenerate the graph in CI and run hosted path-diff alerts after every control-plane, context, connector, or evidence-pack change.

residual_risks.risk: A graph-visible path can still fail if the enforcing runtime ignores policy or allows token passthrough.
residual_risks.treatment: Treat graph output as a diligence and review surface; require MCP gateway, OAuth audience binding, short-lived identity, and egress enforcement at runtime.

implementation: Exposure graph source profile under data/assurance.
CI refresh and validation before the Hugo build.
selected_feature.reason: Agentic security is becoming an exposure-management problem: enterprises need to see how context, identities, MCP tools, authorization, egress, and evidence combine into action paths.

standards_alignment.coverage: Agent interoperability, community protocols, authentication, identity infrastructure, and security evaluations for human-agent and multi-agent interactions.
standards_alignment.name: NIST AI Agent Standards Initiative

standards_alignment.coverage: Agent goal hijack, tool misuse, identity abuse, supply-chain risk, unexpected code execution, memory and context poisoning, insecure inter-agent communication, cascading failures, human-agent trust exploitation, and rogue-agent behavior.
standards_alignment.name: OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications 2026

standards_alignment.coverage: OAuth 2.1, protected resource metadata, resource indicators, audience binding, PKCE, short-lived tokens, refresh-token rotation, HTTPS, and secure token storage for protected MCP servers.
standards_alignment.name: Model Context Protocol Authorization Specification

standards_alignment.coverage: Token exposure, scope creep, tool poisoning, command execution, intent-flow subversion, authorization gaps, audit gaps, shadow MCP servers, and context over-sharing.
standards_alignment.name: OWASP MCP Top 10

standards_alignment.coverage: Identity, authorization, orchestration, runtime behavior, visibility, accountability, and trust for autonomous agents operating across digital environments.
standards_alignment.name: CSA Securing the Agentic Control Plane

standards_alignment.coverage: Agent discovery, Agent Card security, server identity, authentication, authorization, and task exchange for secure agent interoperability.
standards_alignment.name: Agent2Agent Protocol Specification

standards_alignment.coverage: Generative AI provenance, third-party dependency management, monitoring, measurement, data boundary, and incident-response risk actions.
standards_alignment.name: NIST AI RMF Generative AI Profile
